blocks.content: Plain Parquet is immutable — you can't update or delete rows. Every write appends new files. Without a transaction layer you get: partial writes (job fails mid-write → corrupt data), no atomic updates, read inconsistency during concurrent writes, and no schema enforcement. Delta Lake solves all of this.

rows: A write either fully succeeds or fully fails — no partial state
Transaction log entries are written as a commit only after all data files are written
Schema is always valid — no mixed schemas mid-table
Schema enforcement at write time; schema evolution via explicit ALTER
Concurrent readers and writers don't see each other's partial state
Snapshot isolation — readers see a consistent snapshot, writers work in isolation
Committed data is not lost even if the cluster crashes
Data files + transaction log on durable object storage (S3, ADLS)

blocks.content: Every Delta table has a _delta_log/ directory alongside the data files. Every transaction creates a JSON commit file recording what was added, removed, and what the new metadata is. The current state of the table = replaying all commits.

# Delta table directory structure:
# s3://bucket/my_table/
# ├── _delta_log/
# │   ├── 00000000000000000000.json   ← first write (adds part-0.parquet)
# │   ├── 00000000000000000001.json   ← second write (adds part-1.parquet)
# │   ├── 00000000000000000002.json   ← DELETE (removes part-0.parquet)
# │   └── 00000000000000000010.json   ← checkpoint (snapshot of state at version 10)
# ├── part-00000.parquet
# ├── part-00001.parquet
# └── ...

# Every JSON commit contains:
# { "add": { "path": "part-001.parquet", "size": 12345, "stats": {...} } }
# { "remove": { "path": "part-000.parquet", "deletionTimestamp": 1700000000 } }
# { "metaData": { "schema": "...", "partitionColumns": [...] } }
Delta checkpoints every 10 commits by default (Parquet snapshot of the log state). This prevents Spark from replaying thousands of JSON files to reconstruct the current table state.

Because the entire modification history is in the transaction log, you can read any past version of the table. This is called time travel. Invaluable for debugging, auditing, and rolling back bad writes.

# Read table at a specific version number
df_v3 = spark.read.format('delta') \
    .option('versionAsOf', 3) \
    .load('s3://bucket/my_table')

# Read table as it was at a specific timestamp
df_yesterday = spark.read.format('delta') \
    .option('timestampAsOf', '2024-01-15 12:00:00') \
    .load('s3://bucket/my_table')

# SQL syntax
spark.sql("""
    SELECT * FROM my_delta_table VERSION AS OF 3
""")
spark.sql("""
    SELECT * FROM my_delta_table TIMESTAMP AS OF '2024-01-15'
""")

# View full history
spark.sql('DESCRIBE HISTORY my_delta_table').show(truncate=False)
Time travel only works as long as the old data files haven't been vacuumed. Delta's default retention is 7 days. Run VACUUM my_table RETAIN 168 HOURS to enforce this.

MERGE INTO is the most powerful Delta operation. It performs an upsert (update if exists, insert if not) in a single atomic transaction. This is the standard pattern for applying Change Data Capture (CDC) feeds from databases.

# Apply a CDC feed (new updates) to a target Delta table
from delta.tables import DeltaTable

target = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, 's3://bucket/customers')

target.alias('t').merge(
    source=new_records_df.alias('s'),
    condition='t.customer_id = s.customer_id'  # join key
).whenMatchedUpdate(set={
    't.name':       's.name',
    't.email':      's.email',
    't.updated_at': 's.updated_at'
}).whenNotMatchedInsert(values={
    'customer_id': 's.customer_id',
    'name':        's.name',
    'email':       's.email',
    'updated_at':  's.updated_at'
}).execute()

# Can also add: .whenMatchedDelete(condition='s.is_deleted = true')

rune_cards.definition: Directory containing JSON commit files that record every change to a Delta table. Each commit lists added files, removed files, and metadata changes. The table's current state = replaying all commits. Checkpointed every 10 commits.

Delta's concurrency model: each reader sees a consistent point-in-time snapshot of the table even while writes are happening. Achieved via optimistic concurrency control — writers check for conflicts at commit time.

Reading a Delta table at a past version or timestamp using .option('versionAsOf', N) or .option('timestampAsOf', 'date'). Works as long as old data files haven't been vacuumed (default 7-day retention).

Atomic upsert operation: UPDATE matched rows, INSERT new rows, optionally DELETE rows — all in one transaction. Primary tool for applying CDC feeds. Uses the Delta transaction log to ensure atomicity.

quiz.question: After a bad batch ETL job corrupts the last 3 versions of your Delta table, what's the fastest recovery?
options: Re-run the entire ETL from source
Use RESTORE TABLE my_table TO VERSION AS OF N (the last good version)
Delete all Parquet files and rebuild from scratch
quiz.feedback: Delta's RESTORE command re-writes the transaction log to point back to a previous version in a single atomic operation. This is one of the key Delta interview talking points — instant table restore without re-processing source data.

quiz.question: In a Delta MERGE INTO operation, what happens if the source has a row with customer_id=42, but the target table doesn't have customer_id=42?
options: The merge fails with an error
The whenNotMatchedInsert clause fires and inserts the row
The row is written to a quarantine table
quiz.feedback: MERGE INTO has three clauses for three cases: whenMatchedUpdate (row exists in both), whenNotMatchedInsert (row exists in source only), and whenMatchedDelete (optional — delete matched rows). The correct clause fires automatically based on the match condition.
